title: Introduction to Finality

jokes.text: I miss Troy. Wrong, and stop guessing that.
jokes.setup: Student gives wrong answer to biology question
jokes.punchline: Teacher reveals student has been repeatedly guessing 'I miss Troy' as answer
jokes.explanation: Establishes Troy's absence as emotional anchor while subverting academic setting. Economical and character-driven.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown Teacher
dialogue.line: What is the primary function of mitochondria?

dialogue.speaker: Unknown Student
dialogue.line: I miss Troy.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown Teacher
dialogue.line: That's... not correct. And frankly, I'm starting to think you're not actually answering the questions anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown Student
dialogue.line: I miss Troy.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown Teacher
dialogue.line: This is the seventh time this semester you've given that exact answer. To completely different questions. About completely different topics.

jokes.text: Sorry, not 'play.' 'Render imaginated dreamscapes.'
jokes.setup: Annie mentions Abed won't 'play' in the Dreamatorium
jokes.punchline: Self-corrects with absurdly pretentious terminology
jokes.explanation: Perfect Annie character moment - her need to be technically correct leads to ridiculous over-specification.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Abed won't play in the Dreamatorium.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: I mean, he refuses to participate in the postmodern metanarrative construct that is the Dreamatorium.

jokes.text: Did someone say 'sexy construction worker'?
jokes.setup: Serious conversation about Abed and Troy
jokes.punchline: Dean completely misinterprets context for costume opportunity
jokes.explanation: Classic Dean Pelton - his costume obsession overwhelms all context. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: I heard about Abed and Troy. That's a real shame. You know what? I'm going to dress up as a priest and give them some spiritual guidance.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Wait, they're not breaking up? They're just having an argument? Well, I'm already in the outfit. I'm going with it.

jokes.text: I'm dressed like this because I'm building to big news.
jokes.setup: Dean appears in construction worker outfit
jokes.punchline: Elaborate costume justified by terrible pun on 'building' news
jokes.explanation: Weak pun but perfectly in character for Dean's elaborate costume justifications.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: I'm here to deliver some building news.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: And I'm dressed for the occasion because I wanted to... construct my appearance around the message.

jokes.text: But there's only one dotted line on the form, so... / Me. / Hey!
jokes.setup: Shirley suggests they both sign as 50-50 partners
jokes.punchline: Pierce immediately claims sole ownership, surprising Shirley
jokes.explanation: Sharp character moment showing Pierce's selfishness with good timing

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: We should both sign as 50-50 partners.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm going to claim sole ownership.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: What?! Pierce, we agreed!

jokes.text: What's the worst that could happen? Classic tee-up.
jokes.setup: Britta asks rhetorical question about therapy
jokes.punchline: Jeff immediately recognizes it as dramatic irony setup
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-comedy - character acknowledges story structure while it's happening. Classic Community self-awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Isn't therapy just a way for people to avoid actually changing?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Oh wow, you're really setting yourself up there, aren't you?

jokes.text: Shut up, Leonard, I know about your crooked wang.
jokes.setup: Leonard makes sarcastic comment about 'no such thing as bad press'
jokes.punchline: Jeff shuts him down with oddly specific personal attack
jokes.explanation: Part of beloved running gag. 'Crooked wang' is memorably absurd and specific.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: There's no such thing as bad press.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Leonard, that only works if people know who you are. You could walk into a Starbucks covered in blood holding a chainsaw and the barista would just think you were quirky.

jokes.text: This can't be good.
jokes.setup: Jeff sees whatever Abed is doing (implied to be disturbing)
jokes.punchline: Massive understatement delivered deadpan
jokes.explanation: Simple reaction shot comedy. Economy of language works but nothing special.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Abed, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I'm making a human centipede out of the study group.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: That's... not ideal.

jokes.text: We boring you? I miss my friends.
jokes.setup: Troy appears distracted in technical class
jokes.punchline: Honest emotional response to sarcastic question
jokes.explanation: Troy's childlike honesty creates awkward moment. Good character beat showing his emotional state.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, I wasn't listening. What did you say?

dialogue.speaker: AC Teacher
dialogue.line: Were you even paying attention?

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: No, not really. I've been thinking about Abed all morning.

jokes.text: Troy, there is a prophesy. I miss Abed so much.
jokes.setup: Dramatic moment about prophesy
jokes.punchline: Troy completely ignores grand statement to express feelings
jokes.explanation: Troy's emotional honesty disrupts serious moment. Character-driven humor showing his priorities.

dialogue.speaker: Vice Dean Laybourne
dialogue.line: You are the one spoken of in the ancient prophecy. You will bring balance to the air conditioning system.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: I have feelings for Britta.

jokes.text: Troy, there is a prophesy. / I miss Abed so much.
jokes.setup: Laybourne dramatically announces a prophecy
jokes.punchline: Troy completely ignores it, still focused on missing his friend
jokes.explanation: Perfect juxtaposition of grandiose prophecy with emotional honesty

dialogue.speaker: Vice Dean Laybourne
dialogue.line: A great darkness is coming. The signs are all around us. Only one of pure heart can prevent the catastrophe.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Have you seen Abed?

jokes.text: Troy, there is a prophesy. / I miss Abed so much.
jokes.setup: Vice Dean begins epic prophecy
jokes.punchline: Troy ignores it completely to express missing his friend
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment where Troy's genuine emotion cuts through absurd fantasy

dialogue.speaker: Vice Dean
dialogue.line: I foresee a great darkness descending upon Greendale. The walls will crumble, the very foundations of our institution will shake, and chaos will reign supreme.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's cool. Hey, have you seen Abed? I miss hanging out with that guy.

jokes.text: It's a trade school. It's a two-year degree in boxes that make rooms cold.
jokes.setup: Grandiose speech about destiny and repairing men
jokes.punchline: Troy reduces mystical calling to mundane reality
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of pompous mysticism with practical reality. Troy's grounded perspective cuts through absurdity beautifully.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: My destiny is clear. I am meant to repair men, to restore their broken spirits and guide them toward enlightenment.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: You mean like fixing their cars?

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: No, Abed, it's about spiritual renewal and—

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Because you're going to be an air conditioning repair man.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: What? No. I'm talking about a higher calling.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Cool. Cool cool cool. You start Monday.

jokes.text: I'm reading the novelization of Chronicles of Riddick. Can you honestly tell me that's a saner decision?
jokes.setup: Evil Abed criticizes regular Abed for talking to Britta
jokes.punchline: Regular Abed points out his own questionable choices
jokes.explanation: Abed's self-awareness about his pop culture obsessions. Specific reference adds authenticity.

dialogue.speaker: Evil Abed
dialogue.line: You're talking to Britta? That's a mistake.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Says the guy who's evil.

jokes.text: When the world gets bad enough, Abed, the good go crazy. But the smart... They go bad.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Abed's mental state
jokes.punchline: Ominous philosophy delivered with dramatic pause
jokes.explanation: Memorable dark philosophy that captures Evil Abed's worldview. Great dramatic timing and quotable structure.

dialogue.speaker: Evil Abed
dialogue.line: You think you know me? You think you understand what I am? I am the darkness between the frames, the truth behind the fourth wall. And when this show ends, I'll be waiting in the spaces where reality bends.

jokes.text: Hard to break the word 'yes' into lilting syllables, huh? Ye-es?
jokes.setup: Jeff catches Shirley lying about wanting biology help
jokes.punchline: Demonstrates how awkward it would be to lie in her sing-song style
jokes.explanation: Clever observation about Shirley's speech patterns making deception difficult. Good structure and character specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You don't actually want biology help, do you?

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: No, not really.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: So why'd you lie about it?

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: Because I didn't want to hurt your feelings! But look how awkward this is now.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: ♪ I lied, I lied, I told a fib so wide! ♪ See? Terrible!

jokes.text: My biology final is at 5:00. / I'm available from 3:00 to 4:00. / I'll see you in court.
jokes.setup: Legal conflict scheduling around Annie's needs
jokes.punchline: Jeff's casual availability window followed by Pierce's dramatic exit
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Jeff's casual approach vs Pierce's drama

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I'm free Tuesday afternoon.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not. I have a very important appointment.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: What appointment?

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I'm going to go home and die.

jokes.text: I was born in a courtroom, sued it for my own placenta, and won.
jokes.setup: Dean explains his legal credentials
jokes.punchline: Absurd escalation from birth to legal victory over own birth
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd escalation. The specificity of 'placenta' and logical impossibility creates perfect surreal comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: I have a law degree from Colombia. Not the country, the university. Well, actually it's a certificate I got online for $400, but it's from Colombia University online, which counts.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: I used it to sue my own parents for my birth. Turns out I won. I'm now legally my own father.

jokes.text: A-ha! Crazy? Paranoid? Impotent? / Cellular... / I knew you'd run to Jeff, and turn him against me. / Mitosis.
jokes.setup: Pierce overhears Jeff's advice about his paranoia
jokes.punchline: Jeff's desperate attempts to return to biology study
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with Jeff's frustrated focus on biology as running gag

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Pierce, you're not paranoid. Nobody's out to get you.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: That's exactly what someone who's out to get me would say.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Okay, I'm going back to studying biology now.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Biology? That's the study of life. Life is what they use to spy on you.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I really need to focus on this exam.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Exams are just another way the government tracks your knowledge level.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Pierce, please. I'm begging you. Just... let me study.

jokes.text: May his breeze join the wind.
jokes.setup: Announcement of Vice Dean's death from freon inhalation
jokes.punchline: Troy offers air conditioning-themed eulogy
jokes.explanation: Perfectly themed condolence that treats HVAC like a religion. Original and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: The Vice Dean was a man of many talents. He could negotiate a contract, he could lead a meeting, and apparently, he could inhale freon like it was going out of style. But you know what? At least he died doing what he loved. And that was... being really, really cold.

jokes.text: Is that the cold-blooded former colleague that got you fired from your old law firm by ratting on you to the state bar? Tee.
jokes.setup: Jeff recognizes Alan and appears confident
jokes.punchline: Annie's detailed knowledge deflates Jeff's confidence with tennis-scoring sound effect
jokes.explanation: Annie's research thoroughness undermines Jeff's bravado. The 'Tee' sound effect adds whimsical touch.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Alan! Hey, man. I got this.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Actually, did you know that tennis scoring comes from clock faces? In medieval times, they used the 60-minute clock face and moved the hand a quarter turn for each point, which is why it goes 0, 15, 30, 45, and then game. It's called deuce when the score is tied at 40-40 because 'deuce' comes from the French 'deux,' meaning two, and—

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Okay, I don't got this.

jokes.text: Hawthorne's a cash cow, and old a-con here is tugging at that teat. That's money. Money. Please stop that.
jokes.setup: Alan explains Pierce's value to the firm using crude metaphor
jokes.punchline: Makes milking gestures that Jeff finds disturbing
jokes.explanation: Crude physical comedy. Gets reaction through discomfort but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Alan
dialogue.line: You see, Pierce is like a cow. We milk him for all he's worth.

dialogue.speaker: Alan
dialogue.line: And when he's all dried up, we send him to the glue factory.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Okay, that's... that's disturbing.

jokes.text: Hey, just go easy on Shirley. / Scout's honor, Sinead O'Connor.
jokes.setup: Jeff asks Alan to go easy in court
jokes.punchline: Alan's promise references Jeff's baldness with pop culture comparison
jokes.explanation: Decent insult with pop culture reference, though not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Go easy on me in court, okay?

dialogue.speaker: Alan
dialogue.line: Don't worry, I'll be gentle. I promise I won't treat you like you're Lex Luthor's stunt double.

jokes.text: Judging Amy or Judge Judy?
jokes.setup: Dean needs help making important judicial decisions
jokes.punchline: Decision is which TV judge show to emulate
jokes.explanation: Dean's priorities are completely wrong but perfectly in character. Economical setup and payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: I need help making an important judicial decision.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Should I be more like Judge Judy or Judge Dredd?

jokes.text: You might call it the Britta of timelines, where everything is the worst.
jokes.setup: Evil Abed describes the darkest timeline
jokes.punchline: Uses Britta's name as synonym for 'worst'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant use of established running gag. Turns character's name into general descriptor for failure.

dialogue.speaker: Evil Abed
dialogue.line: In the darkest timeline, we use 'Britta' as a verb meaning to screw something up.

dialogue.speaker: Evil Abed
dialogue.line: As in, 'Oh no, I Britta'd it.'

jokes.text: Tell me about your parents, Britta. Um... No?
jokes.setup: Evil Abed tries to therapist-manipulate Britta
jokes.punchline: Britta's confused, questioning refusal
jokes.explanation: Britta's awkward resistance to manipulation. Simple but effective character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Evil Abed
dialogue.line: You know what your problem is, Britta? You're afraid of commitment. You sabotage every relationship because deep down, you don't think you deserve to be happy.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Wait, what? That's not... I mean, that's not even close to—why are you doing a therapist voice?

dialogue.speaker: Evil Abed
dialogue.line: I'm not doing a voice. I'm just being honest. You need to confront your abandonment issues.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Okay, first of all, you're literally evil. Second, I'm the therapist here, remember? And third... am I actually doing that?

jokes.text: Would you think less of Shirley Bennett if she offered to sell Ben on craigslist for a chance to bump it with Denzel? That was a joke for my cousin.
jokes.setup: Alan tries to discredit Shirley with Facebook post
jokes.punchline: Absurd joke that Shirley weakly tries to explain away
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable courtroom drama. Shirley's weak excuse makes it funnier but setup is somewhat contrived.

jokes.text: I thought you said you were gonna go easy. / Felt easy to me.
jokes.setup: Jeff calls out Alan for being harsh
jokes.punchline: Alan's dismissive response shows his callousness
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Alan's sociopathic nature

jokes.text: You're average, Britta Perry. You're every kid on the playground that didn't get picked on. You're a business casual potted plant, a human white sale. You're VH1, Robocop 2, and Back to the Future 3. You're the center slice of a square cheese pizza.
jokes.setup: Evil Abed psychologically attacks Britta
jokes.punchline: Escalating series of 'mediocre' comparisons ending with pizza slice
jokes.explanation: Masterful escalating insult sequence. Each comparison is perfectly crafted to suggest bland mediocrity. The pizza slice ending is unexpectedly specific and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Evil Abed
dialogue.line: You're mediocre. Your psychology degree will be mediocre. Your career will be mediocre.

dialogue.speaker: Evil Abed
dialogue.line: You know what else is mediocre? A frozen pizza slice left out for three days.

dialogue.speaker: Evil Abed
dialogue.line: You're worse than that pizza slice.

jokes.text: Actually, that sounds delicious. I'm the center slice of a square cheese pizza.
jokes.setup: Evil Abed's devastating insults culminating in pizza comparison
jokes.punchline: Britta completely misses the point and finds it appealing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Britta moment - she completely misunderstands the insult and embraces it. Great character consistency and misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Evil Abed
dialogue.line: You're like a pizza cutter. All the way around the pizza, never touching the pizza.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: That's cool. I'd be a pizza cutter.

jokes.text: You're Jim Belushi. Ah. 12%
jokes.setup: Series of mediocrity insults
jokes.punchline: Jim Belushi reference finally lands, Evil Abed notes timeline darkness percentage
jokes.explanation: Jim Belushi as ultimate mediocrity insult is culturally specific and effective. The percentage tracking adds meta-comedy layer.

dialogue.speaker: Evil Abed
dialogue.line: You're like Jim Belushi.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: What does that mean?

dialogue.speaker: Evil Abed
dialogue.line: I don't know. Nobody does. But everyone agrees it's bad.

dialogue.speaker: Evil Abed
dialogue.line: This timeline is now 47% darker.

jokes.text: You're a business casual potted plant, a human white sale. You're VH1, Robocop 2, and Back to the Future 3.
jokes.setup: Continuing devastating assessment of Britta
jokes.punchline: Series of metaphors for mediocrity and disappointing sequels
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating series of metaphors for mediocrity, perfectly crafted

dialogue.speaker: Evil Abed
dialogue.line: You're like a paint-by-numbers painting of a real person. You're like a Blockbuster Video in 2010. You're like a Nickelback album that somehow got worse. You're the human equivalent of a sequel nobody asked for, and not even a good one—you're like Die Hard 5.

jokes.text: Does anybody know any funny jokes? I do. An Irish and a Jew walk into a Chinese laundry... With a gay duck.
jokes.setup: Jeff admits he's unprepared and asks for jokes
jokes.punchline: Pierce offers nonsensical stereotype setup that goes nowhere
jokes.explanation: Meta-comedy about bad comedy. Pierce's incompetent joke attempt is perfectly in character for his problematic humor.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I'm completely unprepared. Does anyone have any jokes?

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Why did the Mexican drive a lowrider to the bank?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I don't know, why?

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Because he wanted to make a de-pasta!

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: That doesn't even make sense.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I know, right? I don't really understand my own jokes.

jokes.text: All right, okay! Oh, right! So you're telling me they're not good at basketball?
jokes.setup: Pierce continues his incomprehensible joke
jokes.punchline: Ends with racist basketball stereotype completely unrelated to setup
jokes.explanation: Intentionally bad and offensive Pierce humor. Works as character beat but joke itself is purposefully terrible.

jokes.text: In the name of the five winds... East, West, North, the one we keep secret, and South
jokes.setup: Formal ceremony beginning
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous mystical air conditioning ritual with 'secret' wind
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist world-building. The 'secret wind' is perfectly mysterious and ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: We are gathered here today for the sacred AC Awareness Ceremony, a tradition passed down through generations of Greendale leadership.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Now, I will perform the ancient ritual to ensure optimal air flow throughout our institution.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: First, we must acknowledge the spirits of the vents. Oh, mighty cooling forces, hear our plea.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: We invoke the secret wind—the wind that flows only through the ducts of enlightened institutions.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: By the power vested in me as Dean, I hereby activate the sacred breath of Greendale.

jokes.text: And I have the right to challenge him... In the sun chamber. Holy crap.
jokes.setup: Troy challenges corrupt leader
jokes.punchline: Invokes deadly trial by combat, everyone reacts with shock
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and group reaction. Sets up absurd trial by combat scenario effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: I invoke the deadly trial by combat!

dialogue.speaker: Everyone
dialogue.line: What?! No! Troy, what are you doing?!

jokes.text: And we use this room for yoga on weekends.
jokes.setup: Dramatic expulsion ceremony from sacred chamber
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of mundane weekend use
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of dramatic moment with mundane reality - excellent economy

dialogue.speaker: AC Repair Student
dialogue.line: I was expelled from the sacred chamber for using it to store my air conditioning supplies on weekends.

jokes.text: Miss Daisy's in the house, thanks for the ride, sorry about slavery.
jokes.setup: Alan threatens Jeff's career prospects
jokes.punchline: Makes inappropriate Driving Miss Daisy reference about being driven to threaten Jeff
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable and inappropriate reference that reveals Alan's character but isn't particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Alan
dialogue.line: I'm going to drive you right into unemployment. Driving Miss Daisy style.

jokes.text: That shark thing was not a metaphor. I'm the big dog now.
jokes.setup: Alan explains how Ted lost the firm he founded
jokes.punchline: Reveals sharks literally ate Ted, then switches to dog metaphor
jokes.explanation: Absurd literal interpretation of business metaphor followed by immediate new animal metaphor. Good structure.

dialogue.speaker: Alan
dialogue.line: Ted lost the firm he founded because sharks literally ate him.

dialogue.speaker: Alan
dialogue.line: Well, not literal sharks. I meant it like, you know, sharks in business—cutthroat competitors.

jokes.text: You can if you're dead. That shark thing was not a metaphor.
jokes.setup: Jeff questions how Ted could lose his own firm
jokes.punchline: Alan reveals the shark metaphor was literal death
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist escalation that subverts metaphor expectations

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: How could Ted lose his own firm?

dialogue.speaker: Alan
dialogue.line: The shark metaphor was literal. Ted is dead.

jokes.text: Blind Justice presiding... aah! Oh, my God! Ahh! Oh, one more recess. I hit the corner right on a fresh tattoo.
jokes.setup: Dean enters dramatically as Blind Justice
jokes.punchline: Immediately injures himself on furniture, reveals he has tattoos
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy undermining Dean's dramatic entrance. The tattoo reveal adds unexpected character detail.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: I am Blind Justice!

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Ah! My shin! Why is there a chair here?

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Wait, you can see my tattoos? I thought the blindfold was opaque!

jokes.text: Jeez, Dennis, are you on coke? Take that crap off and sit down.
jokes.setup: Elaborate ceremony and costume
jokes.punchline: Casual, annoyed shutdown of over-the-top behavior
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of pompous ceremony. Casual drug reference and costume criticism work well together.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Elaborate ceremony and costume

dialogue.speaker: Jeff Winger
dialogue.line: Casual, annoyed shutdown of over-the-top behavior

jokes.text: Evil Abed, what are you doing? I'm going to cut off Jeff Winger's arm. I won't let you. That's horrible. Don't you mean dark? Welcome to my world.
jokes.setup: Abed confronts Evil Abed about violent plan
jokes.punchline: Evil Abed corrects 'horrible' to 'dark' and welcomes Abed to darkness
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Evil Abed's philosophy. The word correction reveals his different moral framework.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: That's a horrible plan.

dialogue.speaker: Evil Abed
dialogue.line: Dark. It's a dark plan. And welcome to the dark side.

jokes.text: Oh, no. Have you been watching Dance Moms again?
jokes.setup: Britta expresses despair about everything being terrible
jokes.punchline: Annie assumes it's from watching reality TV
jokes.explanation: Nice character moment showing Annie's awareness of Britta's habits

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Everything is terrible. The world is falling apart.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Have you been watching reality TV again?

jokes.text: Your honor... I have no closing statement, because I'm throwing the case.
jokes.setup: Expectation of standard lawyer closing argument
jokes.punchline: Jeff announces he's deliberately losing
jokes.explanation: Strong misdirection that subverts legal drama expectations. Sets up Jeff's moral speech effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, I stand before you today to deliver a closing argument that will convince you beyond a shadow of a doubt that my client is innocent. I will present irrefutable evidence, logical reasoning, and the kind of legal expertise that you simply cannot argue with.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: However, I've decided to throw this case. Deliberately. I'm going to lose on purpose.

jokes.text: I have no closing statement, because I'm throwing the case.
jokes.setup: Jeff begins what should be his closing argument
jokes.punchline: Immediately admits he's abandoning his client
jokes.explanation: Strong misdirection that begins Jeff's character transformation moment

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Your Honor, I'd like to present a closing argument on behalf of my client...

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what? I'm abandoning this case. My client is guilty as sin.

jokes.text: Helping only ourselves is bad, and helping each other is good.
jokes.setup: Jeff's elaborate philosophical buildup
jokes.punchline: Reduces complex morality to kindergarten-level truth
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jeff character growth moment. Takes cynical lawyer's complex philosophy and reduces it to simple moral truth.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: You know, morality is this complex tapestry of competing philosophical frameworks, existential considerations, and societal paradigms that have evolved over millennia. We have Kant, we have utilitarianism, we have virtue ethics, all wrestling with the fundamental question of what it means to—

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Sharing is caring.

jokes.text: Why didn't you just do some inspirational speech about friendship? You're fired.
jokes.setup: Jeff gives heartfelt speech about morality
jokes.punchline: Pierce dismisses it as generic sitcom trope, Alan fires Jeff
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary on sitcom conventions. Pierce recognizes the genre while Alan delivers consequence.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: That's the most generic sitcom speech I've ever heard. Next you'll be telling us we all learned something today.

dialogue.speaker: Alan
dialogue.line: You're fired, Jeff.

jokes.text: By the way, never got a chance to tell you this, but it was me who turned you in to the state bar. I know. And I never got a chance to thank you.
jokes.setup: Alan reveals his betrayal as final insult
jokes.punchline: Jeff thanks him instead of being hurt
jokes.explanation: Perfect character growth payoff. Jeff's transformation makes Alan's intended cruelty backfire completely.

dialogue.speaker: Alan
dialogue.line: I betrayed you.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Thank you.

jokes.text: This place has made you so gay. Hey! Don't use 'gay' as a derogatory term. Booyah, good person.
jokes.setup: Alan uses 'gay' as insult
jokes.punchline: Jeff corrects him and celebrates being a good person
jokes.explanation: Shows Jeff's moral growth through progressive values. The 'booyah' celebration is endearing character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Alan
dialogue.line: That's so gay.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Hey, don't use 'gay' as an insult.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Gay is a beautiful thing. I'm a good person.

jokes.text: Gentlemen, take this man to the infinite labyrinth of eternal ice. Yes! No! No, take him to the police. He murdered someone. Take him to jail. You guys are weird.
jokes.setup: Dramatic fantasy punishment for murderer
jokes.punchline: Troy points out normal legal system exists, calls them weird
jokes.explanation: Perfect Troy moment - his practical common sense cuts through elaborate fantasy world-building. Great deflation comedy.

dialogue.speaker: AC School Leader
dialogue.line: You have committed a terrible crime. You must face our justice.

dialogue.speaker: Everyone
dialogue.line: Yeah! Justice! Punishment!

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Guys, there's a normal legal system. You're being really weird right now.

jokes.text: Jeff, that was great. You were like a white Blair Underwood.
jokes.setup: Jeff's impressive courtroom speech
jokes.punchline: Shirley compares him to Black actor but reverses the racial descriptor
jokes.explanation: Shirley's well-intentioned but awkward racial comparison. Character-driven humor showing her perspective.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: That was amazing, Jeff! You were like a young Denzel Washington... if Denzel Washington was white.

jokes.text: My God, Pierce! You had your first good idea.
jokes.setup: Pierce suggests Jeff represent both parties
jokes.punchline: Jeff treats basic suggestion as Pierce's first-ever good idea
jokes.explanation: Backhanded compliment that reveals how low expectations are for Pierce. Good character relationship humor.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: You should represent both parties.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: That's actually a good idea, Pierce.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I'm going to remember this moment for the rest of your life.

jokes.text: Yeah, I made a new rule that the air conditioning repair school has to act like a regular school. I can do that because I'm their messiah.
jokes.setup: Troy explains his return from AC school
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions being messiah as qualification for rule-making
jokes.explanation: Perfect Troy line - treats messianic status as casual administrative detail. Great character voice and callback.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: I'm back from AC school, and I think I should be allowed to make the rules now.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: I mean, I'm basically a messiah.

jokes.text: Cool. I went crazy and I wanted to cut off Jeff's arm. I missed you so much, buddy.
jokes.setup: Troy shares his AC school experience
jokes.punchline: Abed casually mentions psychotic episode then expresses affection
jokes.explanation: Perfect Abed - treats psychological breakdown as casual sharing followed by genuine emotion. Excellent character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: I had a psychotic episode in high school.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: But I love you guys.

jokes.text: You're the only kind of shrink I could ever trust. The kind with as little control over my mind as me.
jokes.setup: Abed explains why Britta cured him
jokes.punchline: Backhanded compliment about her incompetence being reassuring
jokes.explanation: Perfect Abed logic - finding comfort in mutual incompetence rather than expertise

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: You know, Britta, the fact that you're so fundamentally incompetent at everything is actually really reassuring. Because if you had managed to accidentally cure me, I'd know the universe has gone completely insane.

jokes.text: Hello, everybody. I'm Leonard. And I'm here today to review Let's Potato Chips. $1.79 at Kroger's.
jokes.setup: End credits scene setup
jokes.punchline: Leonard doing mundane chip review as if it's important content
jokes.explanation: Leonard's earnest review of cheap chips. Absurdity comes from treating mundane product as worthy of formal review.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: Welcome back to another episode of Chip Chat. Today we're reviewing the classic Lay's potato chip. Notice the subtle ridge pattern, designed for optimal dipping surface area. The flavor profile is a delicate balance of salt and potato starch. I give it a solid 7 out of 10.

jokes.text: That one's shaped like a duck. Well, I guess that's definitely a buy.
jokes.setup: Leonard reviewing potato chips seriously
jokes.punchline: Duck-shaped chip becomes decisive factor in recommendation
jokes.explanation: Absurd criteria for product evaluation. Leonard's logic is endearingly bizarre but not exceptional comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: These are adequate potato chips. Crispy, well-salted, good mouthfeel. I'd give them a solid seven out of ten.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: However, this duck-shaped chip changes everything. This is a ten. No, this is an eleven. I'm recommending these chips to everyone I know.

tmdb_overview: Jeff agrees to represent Shirley in a case against Pierce to determine official ownership of their new sandwich shop.
insight: Season finale escalates absurdist chaos to 81.8, delivering 2.64 jokes per minute across 58 gags.
